setup_simulation_howto.user: How do I set up a simulation on the platform?
setup_simulation_howto.ai_response:
## Overview — how to set up a simulation on the Open Brain Platform

Below is a step-by-step, practical workflow you can follow in the platform (design → configure → validate → generate → execute). I include the key fields the shared state expects, common options, and exact guidance for using the available state tools (getstate / editstate / validatestate). If you want, I can create a ready-to-run example configuration for you — tell me which circuit (or circuit id) and what experiment you want.

---

## 1) Plan the simulation (design)
Decide the scientific question and the minimal simulation specification you need:

- Circuit: which circuit entity (circuit id or name) or a custom circuit
- Scale: single / pair / small / microcircuit / region / system / whole_brain
- Simulation length (ms)
- Initial membrane potential (v_init)
- Random seed(s)
- Neuron sets to include (all / excitatory / inhibitory / explicit IDs / predefined node sets)
- Stimuli: current clamps, Poisson inputs, electric fields, spike trains, etc.
- Recordings: soma voltage recordings (dt), or time-windowed recordings
- Synaptic manipulations: disconnect / connect / scale / change Mg or Ca
- Any other initialization (extracellular calcium, magnesium)

Write a short campaign name and description — these are required metadata for a SimulationCampaign.

---

## 2) Map design into the shared simulation config (state)
The platform uses a shared state block named smc_simulation_config (schema components described in the state tool). Core parts you will populate:

- info
  - campaign_name (string)
  - campaign_description (string)

- initialize (required)
  - circuit: either:
    - Circuit object { name, path } or
    - CircuitFromID { id_str: "<circuit UUID>\ }
  - simulation_length (ms)
  - extracellular_calcium_concentration (optional)
  - v_init (mV)
  - random_seed
  - node_set (optional NeuronSetReference)

- neuron_sets (optional; common options)
  - AllNeurons / ExcitatoryNeurons / InhibitoryNeurons / IDNeuronSet / PredefinedNeuronSet / nbS1VPMInputs / nbS1POmInputs
  - Each neuron set may include sample_percentage and sample_seed

- stimuli (optional): supported types include (examples)
  - ConstantCurrentClampSomaticStimulus — amplitude (nA), duration (ms), neuron_set
  - PoissonSpikeStimulus — frequency (Hz), duration (ms), source/target sets, random_seed
  - SinusoidalPoissonSpikeStimulus, OrnsteinUhlenbeckCurrentSomaticStimulus, SpatiallyUniformElectricFieldStimulus, etc.

- synaptic_manipulations (optional)
  - DisconnectSynapticManipulation / ConnectSynapticManipulation / SynapticMgManipulation / ScaleAcetylcholineUSESynapticManipulation

- timestamps (optional)
  - RegularTimestamps or individual SingleTimestamp entries used by stimuli or manipulations

- recordings (optional)
  - SomaVoltageRecording or TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording (set dt and neuron_set)

Note: the state schema enforces required fields in initialize and info. Use the getstate / editstate tools to read and modify these fields.

---

## 3) Use the state tools to create/modify the configuration
Practical sequence:

1. Read current state (always do this first)
   - Tool: getstate (path: "/smc_simulation_config\ or "/")
   - Purpose: avoid overwriting unknown fields

2. Make focused edits
   - Tool: editstate with JSONPatch operations (add/replace)
   - Best practice: update specific paths only (do not replace the whole state)
   - Example patches:
     - Set campaign info:
       - op: eplace\ (or \add") path: "/smc_simulation_config/info/campaign_name\ value: \My test campaign"
       - path: "/smc_simulation_config/info/campaign_description\ value: \Test: compare Poisson drive vs current clamp"
     - Set circuit by id:
       - op: eplace\ path: "/smc_simulation_config/initialize/circuit\ value: { 	ype": \CircuitFromID", \id_str": "<UUID>\ }
     - Set simulation length:
       - op: eplace\ path: "/smc_simulation_config/initialize/simulation_length\ value: 1000.0
     - Add a Poisson stimulus:
       - op: \add\ path: "/smc_simulation_config/stimuli/poisson1\ value: { 	ype": \PoissonSpikeStimulus",  requency": 10.0, \duration": 200.0, andom_seed": 0, \source_neuron_set": { 	ype":\NeuronSetReference",  lock_name":
b_inputs\ }, 	argeted_neuron_set": { 	ype":\NeuronSetReference",  lock_name":	arget_cells\ } }

3. Validate when you expect the config to be complete
   - Tool: validatestate
   - When: after you've filled all required fields (info, initialize.circuit, initialize.simulation_length, etc.)
   - Behavior: returns success or detailed validation errors you must fix with editstate

Important: follow the patch strategy—use small, targeted patches and always call getstate first.

---

## 4) Prepare campaign generation & execution
- Once the smc_simulation_config is valid:
  - The frontend will typically generate a SimulationCampaign (one per parameter grid) and create Simulation records (one per parameter combination). On the platform that is the next step (the backend/frontend will use the validated config to create the campaign).
- Execution:
  - After generation, each Simulation is executed producing SimulationResult(s) — the platform will show execution status and results.

I cannot perform generation/execution for you here, but I can prepare a fully valid config (smc_simulation_config) that you can hand to the frontend to generate and run.

---

## 5) Common example setups (quick templates)
- Single neuron, current clamp:
  - initialize.simulation_length: 1000.0
  - initialize.v_init: -70.0
  - stimuli: ConstantCurrentClampSomaticStimulus with amplitude 0.1 nA, duration 500 ms, neuron_set = AllNeurons
  - recordings: SomaVoltageRecording dt = 0.1 ms

- Small microcircuit, Poisson background:
  - initialize.circuit = CircuitFromID with microcircuit id
  - stimuli: PoissonSpikeStimulus to excitatory population (frequency 5 Hz)
  - synaptic_manipulations: optional SynapticMgManipulation or ScaleAcetylcholineUSESynapticManipulation
  - recordings: TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording for subsets of neurons

If you want a concrete JSON-style config I can fill in fields and validate it using the state tools.

---

## 6) Tips & gotchas
- Always call getstate before editing to avoid overwriting fields you did not intend to change.
- Use targeted JSONPatch operations (editstate). Never replace the entire smc_simulation_config at once.
- Call validatestate once you intend the configuration to be complete — validation returns precise error messages if required fields are missing.
- For neuron sets that reference nodesets or node names, use NeuronSetReference objects (block_name + block_dict_name if necessary).
- The schema accepts many stimulus types. Provide timestamps (Single or Regular) if you want stimuli aligned precisely in time.
- If you plan parameter sweeps, use arrays or FloatRange constructs in the scan config before generation (the frontend uses that to expand the SimulationCampaign).

---

## 7) Next steps — offer
I can:
- Walk you step-by-step and create a sample simulation config for you and validate it in the shared state (I will use getstate → editstate → validatestate). Tell me:
  - Which circuit (name or UUID)?
  - Simulation length and v_init?
  - Which stimulus/recording you want?
- Or provide a ready-to-copy JSON template for a common experiment (single neuron current clamp or small circuit with Poisson input).

Which would you like me to do next? (If you want me to edit the shared state, tell me the circuit id or a short description and I'll start by reading the current state.)

submit_jobs_digital_brain.user: How can I submit jobs and get responses from the Digital Brain? What language or syntax can I use to communicate with it?
submit_jobs_digital_brain.expected_output: you submit simulation jobs through the Workflows > Simulate interface (graphical UI) or programmatically via Jupyter notebooks. The platform also has an AI chat assistant that accepts natural language queries for data exploration and literature search.
submit_jobs_digital_brain.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation for the Digital Brain and how to submit jobs. I'll search the product documentation now.I'll broaden the search to documentation mentioning \digital brain", \simulation execution", or \job submission".I'll search for \simulation\ as a single token.## Short answer — what to use and how it communicates
- You can submit jobs to the Digital Brain either through the web UI (Workflows → Simulate) or programmatically using the **OBI-one** programmatic interface (Python).  
- Programmatic submissions use structured configuration objects that are serialized to SONATA/JSON config files (e.g., **simulation_config.json**, **node_sets.json**). The configuration follows a JSON-schema produced from the model.  
- Execution and orchestration use the platform execution backend (BlueCelluLab / NEURONMPI). Results are returned as standard simulation outputs (voltage traces, spike times, NWB files, HDF5 spike files, reports).

---

## Modes of submission

### 1) Web interface (no-code)
- Use the platform Workflows → Simulate flow for Single Cell or Small Microcircuit simulations.
- Configure all parameters via the schema-driven UI (left column categories → middle panel for parameters → right panel model preview).
- Click the Generate Campaign button to create a simulation campaign and obtain a **campaign ID**.
- In the Simulations tab you can select one or more generated simulations and click Run Simulation (or Run Selected).
- Monitor statuses: **Created**, **Running**, **Completed**, **Error**.
- Download or preview outputs from each completed simulation.

Key UI artifacts and files:
- Campaign ID (links configuration → simulations)
- Simulation outputs available to preview/download (voltage traces, spikes, NWB, reports).

### 2) Programmatic (recommended for automation / parameter scans)
- Use the **OBI-one** Python library and its workflow classes to build and submit simulations.
  - Examples and classes in docs:
    - **MEModelSimulationSingleConfig** and **MEModelSimulationScanConfig** for single-cell
    - **CircuitSimulationSingleConfig** for small circuit simulations
    - **GenerateSimulationTask** / **GenerateSimulationTask** converts OBI-one config → SONATA config
- The programmatic workflow generates SONATA-compatible configuration files:
  - **simulation_config.json** (SONATA simulation configuration)
  - **node_sets.json** (node set definitions)
- The OBI-one programmatic layer exposes a FastAPI service in local examples (docs mention you can launch a FastAPI docs view at http://127.0.0.1:8100 in examples), and the library classes can stage circuits and generate configs for the remote execution backend.

What you author with code:
- A Python object/JSON structure describing:
  - circuit (MEModel or SONATA circuit), node_set to simulate
  - simulation parameters: simulation_length (ms), timestep (ms), v_init (mV), extracellular calcium, seed
  - stimuli: current_clamp, spike-based sources, Poisson, etc.
  - recordings: soma voltage, time-windowed recordings, etc.
  - synaptic manipulations / connection_overrides (for circuit simulations)

---

## Language / syntax / formats you will use
- JSON schema-based configuration (the UI is generated from a JSON schema; programmatic configs are serialized to JSON/SONATA):
  - simulation parameters saved to **simulation_config.json**
  - node sets saved to **node_sets.json**
- SONATA format for circuit/simulation configuration (standard used by OBI-one)
- Python objects / classes when using OBI-one:
  - e.g., **MEModelSimulationSingleConfig**, **CircuitSimulationSingleConfig**, **GenerateSimulationTask**
- API surface (examples/docs):
  - OBI-one also provides example FastAPI service and notebook examples for programmatic interaction
- Standard neuroscience output formats:
  - NWB, HDF5 spike files, time-series voltage traces (platform returns and lets you download/preview)

---

## Typical programmatic workflow (summary)
1. Build a simulation configuration with OBI-one Python classes (or create the equivalent JSON that matches the schema).
2. Generate a simulation campaign (GenerateSimulationTask produces SONATA JSON files: **simulation_config.json**, **node_sets.json**).
3. Submit/run the simulation via the platform execution backend (the UI or programmatic runner will launch BlueCelluLab/NEURONMPI jobs).
4. Monitor status (Created → Running → Completed or Error).
5. Retrieve outputs (voltage traces, spike times, NWB files, reports) from the simulation result page or programmatic download.

---

## What you’ll receive back
- Simulation status updates (Created, Running, Completed, Error)
- Output files:
  - Voltage traces (per recording location)
  - Spike times (HDF5)
  - NWB files (standardized dataset)
  - Reports and analysis files produced by the execution backend
- Simulation metadata (campaign id, simulation id, configuration used)

---

## Useful pointers from the docs (where to look next)
- For programmatic use, consult the OBI-one examples and the classes:
  - MEModelSimulationSingleConfig, CircuitSimulationSingleConfig, GenerateSimulationTask
- For small microcircuit workflows, check examples under examples/E_run_small_microcircuit and examples/C_forms/circuit_simulation in the OBI-one repo (notebook examples).
- Use the web UI for quick iterative tests and to obtain campaign IDs; use OBI-one for automated or large parameter-sweep workflows.

---

If you want, I can:
- Show a minimal Python example that builds an OBI-one config object and writes the SONATA JSON files (using the documented class/field names).  
- Or walk through the web-UI steps for a specific simulation type (single-cell vs small microcircuit). Which would you prefer?

simulate_simple_neuron.user: How do you simulate a neuron on this platform? Can I simulate 1 simple neuron for 1 second?
simulate_simple_neuron.expected_output: Go to Workflows > Simulate > Single Neuron, select an ME-model, set the `max_time` parameter to 1000 ms (1 second) in the Experimental Setup, configure a stimulation protocol and recording location, then click Run. The simulation executes via the small-scale simulator backend and displays voltage traces in the results panel
simulate_simple_neuron.ai_response:
Let me fetch the platform documentation for single-cell simulation so I can give exact steps.## Short answer
Yes — you can simulate a single neuron for 1 second on this platform. The single‑cell simulation workflow supports ME‑models (morphology + electrophysiology), configurable stimuli, and recordings. Set the simulation duration to **1000 ms** (1 s) and choose the stimulus/recording you need.

---

## How to simulate a single neuron — step‑by‑step

### 1) Start a Single Neuron simulation (UI)
- Go to **Workflows → Simulate** and choose **Single Neuron**.
- Select an ME‑model from your project or the public library (an MEModel that includes morphology + emodel).

### 2) Info (required)
- **Name:** give the simulation a unique name (required).
- **Description:** optional.

### 3) Experimental setup (temporal & environment)
Set the simulation timing and environment:
- **Maximum time / Simulation length:** **1000 ms** (this is 1 second).
- **Time step (dt):** default is **0.05 ms** (you can use e.g. 0.025 ms for higher temporal resolution).
- **Initial voltage (v_init):** default **-80 mV** (can change).
- **Temperature (°C), seed, extracellular Ca++**: configurable (defaults provided).

Example configuration:
- **Simulation length:** **1000.0 ms**
- **Timestep:** **0.05 ms**
- **v_init:** **-80 mV**

### 4) Stimulation protocol
- Choose **Stimulus type = current_clamp** (or other types available).
- Choose a protocol (step, AP_waveform, etc.) or create a custom stimulus.
- Specify:
  - **Injection location** (e.g., soma[0] — you can pick this in the 3D viewer)
  - **Amplitude** (nA)
  - **Delay / duration / stop time** — for a simple 1 s run you can e.g. use delay 100 ms, duration 800 ms, stop 1000 ms, or a constant injection active for the time you want.

### 5) Recordings
- Add one or more recording locations (click on the 3D neuron or enter manually).
- Recording options:
  - **SomaVoltageRecording** (voltage)
  - **TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording** (restricted time window)
  - Optionally record ionic currents (adds extra traces).
- Each recording has a color in the plots and can record at specified dt.

### 6) Validate & Run
- The platform validates your configuration and warns about issues or large outputs.
- Click **Run Simulation**. Status will show: launching → running → completed.
- When finished, open the **Results** panel to view voltage traces, spike times, and download outputs (NWB, traces, reports).

---

## Programmatic option (OBI‑one)
You can also configure and run single‑cell simulations programmatically using the OBI‑one API:

- Use the class **MEModelSimulationSingleConfig** to set:
  - circuit (MEModel or MEModelFromID)
  - simulation_length (ms) → set to **1000.0**
  - v_init, timestep, extracellular_calcium_concentration, random_seed
  - stimuli: constant current clamp, Poisson, etc.
  - recordings: SomaVoltageRecording or TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording
- Generate the SONATA config and run via the BlueCelluLab backend (NEURON).
- Example: set simulation_length = 1000.0, timestep = 0.05, add a constant current clamp at soma, add soma voltage recording.

(See the documentation section **OBI-one: Programmatic Single Cell Simulations** for examples and file locations in the repository.)

---

## Practical notes & tips
- 1 second = **1000 ms** — set **Simulation length** to **1000 ms**.
- Choose timestep to balance accuracy vs runtime: smaller dt → more compute/time.
- Recording currents (ionic currents) generates ~6× more traces per recording location.
- The platform warns if expected output >150 MB.
- Use the 3D neuron viewer to pick injection/recording locations precisely.
- Default initial v_init = **-80 mV**, default temperature **34°C**.
- If you need only a very simple test (no external synapses), choose the basic single neuron simulation and a constant current clamp.

---

## Would you like me to…
- Walk you through creating a 1‑s simulation in the UI step‑by‑step (I can prepare exact parameter values), or
- Prepare an OBI‑one configuration snippet (JSON/Python) with simulation_length = **1000 ms** that you can run programmatically?

Tell me which option you prefer and I’ll prepare the next steps.

Below are concrete, tested ways to take an ME-model (an ME-Model entity you have selected) and run simulations using the OBI tooling and example notebooks. I’ll show the recommended notebooks, the key steps, and the example code snippets you can reuse to configure, validate and run ME-Model simulations (including grid scans and single runs). ## Where to find the example notebooks (examples used by the platform)
The OBI-One repository contains ready-to-run example notebooks for ME-Model simulations:

- Example notebook that generates and executes a grid scan for MEModel simulations:
  - Source: examples/F_single_cell_simulations/entitysdk_memodel_simulation.ipynb

- Example notebook that configures and validates an MEModel with synapses simulation:

(These are available in the OBI-One GitHub examples folder; the snippets below are drawn from those notebooks.)

---

## Typical workflow — steps and core operations

1. Prepare your environment
   - Install and import the OBI-One Python package (commonly referenced as obi_one / obi).
   - Ensure you have access rights and a DB client if needed (examples use db_client).

2. Build the simulation form (configure)
   - Use the appropriate form class (e.g., MEModelWithSynapsesCircuitSimulationScanConfig.empty_config()) and populate:
     - Info (campaign name/description)
     - Timestamps (RegularTimestamps)
     - Stimuli (PoissonSpikeStimulus, ConstantCurrentClampSomaticStimulus, etc.)
     - Recording blocks (SomaVoltageRecording, etc.)
     - Initialization block referencing your ME-model ID (e.g., MEModelWithSynapsesCircuitFromID(id_str=entity_ID))

3. Validate the configuration
   - Call validated_config() on the form to ensure the configuration is complete and valid.

4. Wrap into a Simulation / Grid scan (optional)
   - Use GridScanGenerationTask to create a grid scan for parameter sweeps, or generate a single Simulation campaign.

5. Execute the simulation
   - Use the provided run helper to execute with the BlueCelluLab backend (or Neurodamus).
   - Optionally save results (e.g., NWB format).

6. Collect and inspect results
   - Output directory will contain simulation outputs; follow the notebook examples to parse or visualize results.

---

## Key example code snippets (from the OBI-One example notebooks)

- Configure and validate an MEModelWithSynapses simulation:
```python
from pathlib import Path
import obi_one as obi

# === Parameters ===
sim_duration = 3000.0

sim_form = obi.MEModelWithSynapsesCircuitSimulationScanConfig.empty_config()

# Info
info = obi.Info(
    campaign_name=\MEModel With Synapses Simulation",
    campaign_description=\Simulation of example MEModel With Synapses"
)
sim_form.set(info, name=\info")

# Timestamps
timestamps = obi.RegularTimestamps(start_time=0.0, number_of_repetitions=1, interval=100)
sim_form.add(timestamps, name=\Timestamps")

# Stimulus examples
stimulus = obi.PoissonSpikeStimulus(duration=800.0, timestamps=timestamps.ref, frequency=20)
sim_form.add(stimulus, name=\PoissonInput")

stimulus = obi.ConstantCurrentClampSomaticStimulus(timestamps=timestamps.ref, duration=2000.0, amplitude=0.5)
sim_form.add(stimulus, name=\CurrentClampInput")

# Recording
recording = obi.SomaVoltageRecording()
sim_form.add(recording, name=\SomaVoltage")

# Initialization block (reference your ME-model ID here)
init = obi.MEModelWithSynapsesCircuitSimulationScanConfig.Initialize(
    circuit=obi.MEModelWithSynapsesCircuitFromID(id_str=entity_ID),
    simulation_length=sim_duration,
)
sim_form.set(init, name=\initialize")

# Validated Config
validated_sim_conf = sim_form.validated_config()
print(validated_sim_conf)
```

- Wrap the validated configuration into a GridScanGenerationTask and execute:
```python
grid_scan = obi.GridScanGenerationTask(
    form=validated_sim_conf,
    coordinate_directory_option=\ZERO_INDEX",
    output_root='../../../obi-output/memodel_simulations/grid_scan'
)
grid_scan.multiple_value_parameters(display=True)
grid_scan.coordinate_parameters(display=True)
grid_scan.execute(db_client=db_client)    # creates the generated simulations in DB (example)
obi.run_tasks_for_generated_scan(grid_scan, db_client=db_client)
```

- Run a simulation using the BlueCelluLab backend (single-run helper):
```python
from obi_one.scientific.library.simulation_execution import run

run(
    simulation_config=simulation_config_path,
    simulator='bluecellulab',  # or 'neurodamus'
    save_nwb=False             # set True to save NWB output
)
```

---

## Notes & practical tips

- Replace entity_ID in the initialization block with the UUID of the ME-model you selected.
- Use the validated_config() step — it prevents execution failures caused by missing fields.
- Grid scans are recommended when you want parameter sweeps (stimulus amplitude, frequency, etc.). For a single configuration, generate a single Simulation instead.
- BlueCelluLab is the default simulator in the examples; you may select 
eurodamus\ if your setup supports it.
- Example notebooks include additional utility code to inspect outputs, convert outputs to NWB, and visualize traces.

synaptic_input_meaning.user: What does the "synaptic input" on the current page mean? Is it a signal input from a specified position?
synaptic_input_meaning.ai_response:
I'll search the platform glossary for the term \synaptic\ to retrieve the official definition. I'll run a glossary lookup now.## Short answer
**\Synaptic input\** on the page refers to the set of incoming synapses (incoming connection/synapse distributions) provided to the neuron model — i.e., the *incoming synaptic connections* from other neurons or external sources. It is **not** inherently a single point-like \signal input from a specified physical position\ unless the page explicitly shows a synapse set tied to a specific location on the morphology.

## Official platform definition (glossary)
I looked up the platform glossary entry for related terms. Relevant definitions:

- **Synaptome** — *A combination of a neuronal morphology, an electrical model and a set of incoming synaptic connections from other neurons. Each synaptome has different synapse sets each representing synaptic inputs distributions from intrinsic connections (from same circuit) and extrinsic connections (other circuits or brain regions). A synaptome can be simulated on the platform including combinations of different synapse sets available within the synaptome model.*

(From the platform glossary: total items found = 3.)

## What that means in practice
- Synaptic input = a distribution or set of synaptic contacts (locations + properties) that target the neuron model.
- These inputs can be:
  - **Intrinsic** (from neurons inside the same circuit),
  - **Extrinsic** (from other circuits/brain regions), or
  - Multiple synapse sets combined into a synaptome.
- Each synapse in the set typically has a location on the neuron's morphology, but the phrase “synaptic input” on its own usually denotes the *collection* of synapses and their statistical/provenance description (rates, weights, pre-synaptic populations), not a single point-source drive.

## When it might mean a specified position
- If the page displays a labeled synapse set or visualization that shows synapse coordinates (e.g., colored markers on the morphology) or a named nodeset/edge population tied to a particular compartment, then you can interpret those synaptic inputs as coming from those specific positions.
- Otherwise, expect the term to mean the incoming connection set/distribution (with possible per-synapse positions) rather than a single positional input.

## Next steps / I can do for you
- If you want, I can open the specific page or entity you’re looking at and show the exact synapse set details (locations, source population, intrinsic/extrinsic tag). Would you like me to fetch that page/entity now?

run_simulation_file.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation on running circuit / single-cell simulation scripts. I'll fetch relevant product documentation pages now.## How to run a simulation file (e.g., **passive_neuron_1dend.py**) on the platform

Below are two supported ways to run a simulation file on the Open Brain Platform, using the documentation for circuit / small microcircuit simulations and OBI-one workflows.

---

## 1) Using the web interface (recommended for quick runs & users who prefer GUI)

Steps:
1. Go to **Workflows → Simulate** in your project and choose **Small Microcircuit** (or the appropriate simulation type).
2. In **Configuration**:
   - Select or upload your circuit model. For a single-cell ME-model like **passive_neuron_1dend.py**, you may need to use the single-cell workflow or supply the ME-model / SONATA config that corresponds to the script. (The interface expects a SONATA circuit for circuit simulations.)
   - Fill the configuration schema sections (Initialize, neuron_sets, stimuli, recordings, etc.). The interface shows grouped categories on the left and a detailed editor in the middle.
   - Set required fields:
     - **initialize.circuit** — select your circuit (path or entity)
     - **initialize.simulation_length** — duration (ms)
     - **initialize.v_init** — initial voltage (mV)
     - **initialize.node_set** — reference to a biophysical neuron set (for single neuron: the node set containing your neuron)
     - Recordings: add **SomaVoltageRecording** if you want voltage traces
3. Validate the configuration. Fix any schema validation errors shown in red.
4. Click **Generate Campaign**. The platform will create a simulation campaign and generate one or more simulations (one per parameter combination).
5. In the **Simulations** tab:
   - Select the simulation(s) you want to run.
   - Click **Run** to execute them.
   - Monitor status: Created → Running → Completed or Error.
6. After completion, view/download outputs (voltage traces, spike times, NWB/HDF5 result files).

Notes from docs:
- The web UI uses a JSON-schema driven editor and will generate SONATA simulation configurations for execution.
- Small microcircuit interface supports up to 20 biophysical neurons; single-cell / ME-model workflows are available as dedicated workflows in OBI-one if needed.

---

## 2) Programmatic: using **OBI-one** (CLI / Python) — preferred for reproducible script runs

Overview from docs:
- OBI-one provides standardized workflows (classes and tasks) to generate SONATA configs and run simulations programmatically.
- Key classes: **CircuitSimulationSingleConfig**, **GenerateSimulationTask**, and the execution library (obi_one/scientific/library/simulation_execution.py).

Suggested workflow (high-level):

1. Prepare your model
   - If your script is a single-cell Python script (e.g., **passive_neuron_1dend.py**), convert or wrap it into an OBI-one compatible ME-model or SONATA circuit representation the OBI-one tasks expect (OBI-one expects SONATA circuit for circuit sims; single-cell ME-model workflows are available).
   - Alternatively, create a small circuit/SONATA config that includes one biophysical neuron with the morphology / mechanisms used by **passive_neuron_1dend.py**.

2. Create a simulation configuration using OBI-one
   - Use the **CircuitSimulationSingleConfig** (or the specific single-cell config class) to define:
     - circuit: path or CircuitFromID
     - initialize: { simulation_length, v_init, node_set, extracellular_calcium_concentration, random_seed }
     - stimuli: (e.g., current clamp) if needed
     - recordings: SomaVoltageRecording or TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording
   - Example (conceptual; implement in Python using obi_one API):
     - Instantiate the config object and set fields described above.

3. Generate SONATA simulation config files
   - Run **GenerateSimulationTask** to:
     - resolve the circuit
     - write node_sets.json
     - write simulation_config.json (SONATA)
     - stage circuit files if using CircuitFromID

4. Execute the simulation
   - The platform uses a BlueCelluLab backend (NEURON / NEURONMPI). Use the provided execution utilities (obi_one/scientific/library/simulation_execution.py) to run the generated SONATA config.
   - Execution supports distributed runs (MPI) when needed.

5. Collect results
   - Outputs include: voltage traces, spike times, NWB files and HDF5 spike/trace files.
   - OBI-one will produce standard output locations (reports and HDF5/NWB) which you can download.

Practical tips from docs:
- The initialize.node_set must reference a biophysical neuron set.
- Circuit simulations automatically include ProbAMPANMDA_EMS and ProbGABAAB_EMS synaptic mechanisms (relevant if you add synapses).
- Use the example notebooks in the OBI-one repo:
  - examples/E_run_small_microcircuit/
  - examples/C_forms/circuit_simulation/

---

## Checklist (quick reference)

- [ ] Confirm whether **passive_neuron_1dend.py** is:
  - a ready-to-run OBI-one / ME-model file OR
  - a standalone NEURON script that must be adapted to SONATA/OBI-one format.
- [ ] If standalone NEURON script, convert to an OBI-one-compatible model (ME-model or SONATA circuit) or create a SONATA wrapper.
- [ ] Define an initialize block:
  - **circuit**, **node_set**, **simulation_length**, **v_init**, **random_seed**
- [ ] Add recordings (SomaVoltageRecording)
- [ ] Validate configuration (UI will show errors)
- [ ] Generate campaign and run simulations (UI) OR run GenerateSimulationTask + execution (OBI-one)
- [ ] Retrieve outputs (voltage traces, spikes, NWB/HDF5)

---

## Troubleshooting & tips

- If the UI complains that **node_set** is not biophysical: ensure your circuit node population contains a biophysical neuron or provide an ME-model equivalent.
- For reproducibility: set **initialize.random_seed**.
- For long runs or parallel execution, use the OBI-one programmatic path and run with MPI if supported.
- Start with short durations (e.g., 200–1000 ms) to validate the setup before scaling up.

---

## Where to find examples & docs on the platform
- See the OBI-one examples and the documentation pages:
  - **OBI-one: Small Circuit Simulations** (explains classes and generate/execution flow)
  - **Small Microcircuit Simulation (Web Interface)** (explains the three-column configuration UI and campaign lifecycle)
  - Example notebooks in the OBI-one repository: examples/E_run_small_microcircuit/

- The platform provides mature workflows for biophysically detailed neuronal and circuit simulations (OBI-one / SONATA-based), but I could not find documentation for direct 3D anatomical / surgical-planning workflows (mesh import, MRI-to-mesh, FEM biomechanics or CSF hydrodynamics) in the platform docs I searched.  
  - I searched documentation (keywords: **anatomy**, **3D**, **surgical**) and found no dedicated docs or public projects for surgical planning or importing patient anatomy.  
  - The platform DOES document circuit and small/microcircuit simulation workflows (OBI-one), including how to specify a circuit (Circuit or CircuitFromID) and configure simulation parameters and synaptic manipulations.

Conclusion: you can use the platform to simulate neural activity and network-level consequences of altered structure (by modeling altered circuits, node sets, connectivity, or synaptic properties), but the platform documentation does not show built‑in support for converting patient MRI into a surgical‑grade 3D anatomical model (mesh/FEM) or for biomechanics/CSF modeling needed for full surgical planning of a Chiari malformation.

---

## How you could proceed on this platform (practical options)
Below I give two realistic paths you can take depending on your goal and the data you have.

### Path A — Simulate electrophysiological / network consequences of the malformation
Best if your interest is how compression (tonsillar herniation) affects neural activity (brainstem/cerebellar circuits), not biomechanical deformation.

Steps
1. Choose modeling scale:
   - Single-cell / paired neuron — for cellular electrophysiology (OBI-one paired or single-cell).
   - Small microcircuit (3–20 biophysical neurons) — for local circuits (OBI-one small circuit).
   - Region-scale circuit — if you have an atlas-based model or a region circuit model (the platform supports circuit scales that include “region”).

2. Obtain or build a circuit model (SONATA):
   - Use an existing circuit in the platform (Circuit or CircuitFromID) or prepare a SONATA circuit describing the nodes and edges you want to simulate.
   - If no existing model for the posterior fossa/brainstem/cerebellum is available, build a simplified circuit that includes the relevant node populations (e.g., cerebellar Purkinje, deep nuclei, brainstem nuclei).

3. Encode the malformation effects as network/physiological perturbations:
   - Represent mechanical compression as changes in neuronal properties or connectivity rather than geometry:
     - Reduced firing thresholds or elevated v_init, altered extracellular calcium, or altered synaptic release probability to represent ischemia/irritation.
     - Remove or downscale node populations (lost neurons) or reduce connection probabilities to represent damaged tissue.
     - Add synaptic manipulations (e.g., SynapticMgManipulation or ScaleAcetylcholineUSESynapticManipulation) or connection_overrides in the SONATA config (the platform’s OBI-one generators support synaptic manipulations).
   - Use stimuli and recordings to test functional outcomes (e.g., evoked responses, spontaneous firing).

4. Configure & run simulation with OBI-one:
   - Use the OBI-one configuration schema: set initialize.circuit (Circuit or CircuitFromID), initialize.simulation_length, v_init, extracellular_calcium_concentration, random_seed, and node_set to select neurons to simulate.
   - Generate a simulation campaign and run it using the platform’s web interface or programmatic OBI-one API.
   - Analyze outputs: voltage traces, spike times, reports.

Notes from the platform docs (useful fields)
- initialize.circuit can be a Circuit or CircuitFromID (you can reference an entity by ID).
- initialize.simulation_length (ms), v_init (mV), extracellular_calcium_concentration (mM), and node_set are standard initialize parameters.
- Synaptic manipulations available: SynapticMgManipulation, ScaleAcetylcholineUSESynapticManipulation.
- Small circuits are up to 20 biophysical neurons; larger circuits are supported but have different resource demands.

---

### Path B — If you need true 3D anatomy / surgical planning (geometry, mesh, CSF, biomechanics)
- The documentation I found did **not** show platform support for surgical‑planning features such as importing patient MRI/DICOM/NIfTI, generating surface/volume meshes, running FEM structural or fluid (CSF) simulations, or integrated surgical visualization tools.
- Because those capabilities are not documented, the likely options are:
  1. Ask platform support whether mesh / MRI import and biomechanics modules exist or are available as private features.

multi_neuron_simulation.user: How can I run a multi-neuron simulation on the platform?
multi_neuron_simulation.ai_response:
I'll fetch the platform documentation for circuit/circuit-simulation to give precise, platform-backed steps.## How to run a multi‑neuron (circuit) simulation on the Open Brain Platform

Below are concise, platform‑backed instructions (web UI and programmatic OBI‑one) synthesized from the platform documentation.

---

## Quick overview
- Circuit (multi‑neuron) simulations model networks of interconnected biophysical neurons and their synapses.  
- Small microcircuits are defined as up to **20** biophysical neurons; larger circuits are handled but require more resources.  
- Workflow: **Configure → Generate Campaign → Run Simulations → Analyze Results**.

---

## Option A — Web interface (recommended for interactive use)

### 1) Open the simulator
- Go to your Project → Workflows → **Simulate** → choose **Small Microcircuit** (or the appropriate circuit simulation type).

### 2) Build the configuration (Configuration tab)
- Use the three‑column schema UI:
  - Left column: configuration categories (expand/collapse)
  - Middle column: parameter forms for the selected element
  - Right column: **Model preview** (visual + metadata)
- Add and configure elements using the Add button (system generates unique names you can edit).
- Important fields to set in the Initialize block:
  - **circuit** — select a circuit model from your project or public data
  - **node_set** — must reference a *biophysical* neuron set (e.g., AllNeurons or an IDNeuronSet)
  - **simulation_length** — simulation duration (ms)
  - **v_init** — initial membrane potential (mV)
  - **extracellular_calcium_concentration** — (mM) affects release probability
  - **random_seed**, **timestep**, etc.

### 3) Add stimuli and recordings (optional but typical)
- Stimuli types: current clamp (constant, linear, sinusoidal), Poisson spike inputs, synchronous spike stimuli, etc.
- Recordings: SomaVoltageRecording or TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording; spike detection is supported.

### 4) Validate and generate a campaign
- Fix any validation errors shown in red.
- Click **Generate Campaign** (or equivalent). The system validates and creates a **Simulation Campaign** and generates one Simulation per parameter combination.
- Save the generated **campaign ID** (links simulations to the configuration).

### 5) Run simulations (Simulations tab)
- Switch to **Simulations** tab to see the generated simulations and their statuses (Created / Running / Completed / Error).
- Select simulations to run and click **Run**.
- Monitor progress in real time and open outputs when complete.

### 6) Access outputs
- Outputs may include voltage traces, spike times, SONATA config files, NWB files, HDF5 spike files, and other reports.
- Download or preview files directly from the UI.

---

## Option B — Programmatic: OBI‑one (for scripted or batch runs)

### Key components (from OBI‑one)
- Use the **CircuitSimulationSingleConfig** and **GenerateSimulationTask** classes to create SONATA configs programmatically.
- The Circuit class represents a SONATA circuit and contains node/edge populations and node sets.

### Typical programmatic workflow
1. Create a circuit reference (either path to SONATA config or CircuitFromID).
2. Build a **CircuitSimulationSingleConfig** specifying:
   - circuit (Circuit or CircuitFromID)
   - initialize: node_set (must be biophysical), simulation_length, v_init, extracellular_calcium_concentration, random_seed
   - stimuli (optional)
   - recordings (optional)
   - synaptic_manipulations (optional)
3. Run **GenerateSimulationTask** to:
   - resolve the circuit
   - write SONATA simulation_config.json and node_sets.json
   - stage required circuit files
4. Execute the produced SONATA simulation config with the execution backend:
   - BlueCelluLab backend (NEURONMPI) supports distributed runs and automatic neuron distribution across MPI ranks.
5. Collect outputs (voltage traces, spike files, NWB, HDF5 spike files, reports).

Notes:
- Circuit simulations automatically include the platform synaptic mechanisms (e.g., **ProbAMPANMDA_EMS**, **ProbGABAAB_EMS**) and support synaptic manipulations (e.g., magnesium changes or acetylcholine scaling).
- Examples and notebooks are available in the platform examples (e.g., examples/E_run_small_microcircuit/).

---

## Practical tips and best practices
- Start simple: test with a small node set and a short simulation_length to check configuration and performance.
- Validate configuration before generating a campaign. Fix errors reported by the UI.
- Use the Model Preview to confirm the node populations and node sets you intend to simulate.
- Save the campaign ID — it references your configuration and generated simulations.
- Batch runs: generate a campaign that parameterizes the variables you want to sweep, then select and run subsets of the generated simulations.
- Incremental testing: test small parameter ranges before large parameter sweeps to save compute and debugging time.

---

## Where this guidance comes from
- Documentation retrieved from the platform:
  - **Small Microcircuit Simulation (Web Interface)** — product: *circuit-simulation* (describes the UI workflow and configuration).
  - **OBI-one: Small Circuit Simulations** — product: *circuit-simulation* (programmatic API, SONATA config generation, execution backend).
  - **Overview** and **Tips** pages summarizing workflow and best practices.

(If you want, I can open the specific example notebook or show a minimal example JSON/SONATA initialize block based exactly on the platform schema. Which would you prefer next?)

## Summary / next step
I’ll help you design and (optionally) generate simulation configurations for two circuits: **(1) aging mouse hippocampal region** and **(2) adult mouse hippocampal region after traumatic brain injury (TBI)**, then define the comparisons and outputs you’ll need. First I’ll outline recommended design choices, default parameters, and measurable outcomes. After you confirm choices (or change them), I can draft concrete simulation configurations you can run on the platform.

---

## Key choices I need from you (or I’ll apply sensible defaults)
Please confirm or change any of these. If you want me to proceed with defaults, say “Proceed with defaults”.

1. **Circuit scale**
   - Options: **region** (atlas-based hippocampus volume), **microcircuit** (100s–thousands of neurons within hippocampal subfields), **small** (3–20 neurons — not recommended here).
   - Default recommendation: **microcircuit** or **region**. Microcircuit if you want detailed cell-level dynamics and parameter sweeps; region if you want atlas-scale population results.

2. **Hippocampal subregions to include**
   - Options: CA1, CA2, CA3, dentate gyrus (DG), subiculum.
   - Default: **CA1 + CA3 + DG** (common for aging and TBI effects).

3. **Species and ages**
   - Species: *Mus musculus* (mouse) — you already specified.
   - Age groups:
     - **Adult**: ~3–6 months (default for “adult”).
     - **Aged**: ~18–24 months (default for “aging”).
   - Confirm these or provide alternative age ranges.

4. **TBI model specifics**
   - TBI can be modeled in many ways: focal neuron loss, diffuse axonal injury (reduced conduction), altered inhibition/excitation balance, increased synaptic failure probability, inflammatory modulation of excitability.
   - Default TBI model I propose:
     - **20% focal neuron loss** in CA1 (adjustable)
     - **20–30% reduction in long-range/extrinsic excitatory connections**
     - **10–25% change in inhibitory synaptic strength** (commonly reduced GABAergic efficacy)
     - **Increased synaptic failure** (higher short-term depression)
   - Confirm or specify alternative pathophysiology.

5. **Aging model specifics**
   - Common aging effects to include: reduced spine/synapse density, altered channel kinetics (e.g., reduced Ih, altered Ca2+ handling), reduced excitatory drive, increased neuronal excitability variability, reduced inhibitory interneuron numbers.
   - Default aging model I propose:
     - **15–30% reduction in excitatory synapse counts**
     - **10–20% reduction in inhibitory interneuron density or inhibitory synapse strength**
     - **Increased spike-timing variability** and modest changes to intrinsic properties (e.g., small changes in leak/threshold)
   - Confirm or adjust magnitudes.

6. **Model detail for single neurons**
   - Choose between purely point-neuron models (faster) vs morphologically-detailed multicompartmental models (slower, more realistic).
   - Default: **mixed** — morphologically detailed for representative principal neurons and point/multi-compartment reduced models for interneurons to keep simulation tractable.

7. **Simulation runtime & stimuli**
   - Runtime per trial: e.g., **10 s** of simulated time (default) to capture network activity and oscillations.
   - Stimuli: spontaneous background input vs patterned stimulation (e.g., Schaffer collateral activation, theta-burst).
   - Default: **baseline spontaneous activity + episodic excitatory drive** to probe network response.

8. **Number of trials / stochastic seeds**
   - Default: **10 trials** per condition (different random seeds) to capture variance.

9. **Key outputs / metrics to compare**
   - Recommended: firing rates by population, spike raster plots, local field potential (LFP) proxies (population synaptic currents), oscillation power (theta/gamma), pairwise synchrony / cross-correlation, excitatory/inhibitory (E/I) balance, per-cell membrane potential statistics, effective connectivity changes (synapse counts & strengths).
   - Any additional metrics you want (e.g., place cell properties, plasticity measures)?

10. **Parameter sweep / sensitivity analysis**
    - Do you want to sweep TBI severity (e.g., 0–40% neuron loss) or aging severity (e.g., 0–30% synapse loss)?
    - Default: include a 3-point sweep (mild/moderate/severe).

---

## Recommended simulation design (detailed defaults)
- **Scale:** Microcircuit representing CA3 → CA1 → DG sub-circuit (atlas-constrained volume if preferred).
- **Populations and sizes (example microcircuit):**
  - CA3 pyramidal: 800 neurons
  - CA1 pyramidal: 1200 neurons
  - DG granule: 1000 neurons
  - Inhibitory interneurons (per subregion): 15–25% of excitatory counts (modeled as fast-spiking & SOM types)
- **Connectivity**
  - Probabilistic connectivity with distance-dependent weights for local connectivity; include Schaffer collaterals from CA3→CA1 and mossy fiber projections DG→CA3 as appropriate.
  - Baseline synapse counts and strengths taken from platform templates (I can link or use platform archetypes). If you want, I’ll fetch available hippocampal circuit templates on the platform.
- **Aging modifications**
  - Scale excitatory synapse counts: multiply by 0.75 (25% reduction)
  - Scale inhibitory synaptic strength: multiply by 0.85 (15% reduction)
  - Increase membrane noise (σ) by 20%
- **TBI modifications**
  - Remove randomly 20% of CA1 pyramidal neurons (focal lesion)
  - Reduce long-range excitatory connection probability by 25%
  - Reduce inhibitory synapse reliability (increase failure probability)
- **Cell models**
  - Use available E-models or ME-models from the platform for principal neurons; default to reduced multicompartment models if platform templates exist.
- **Simulation**
  - Duration: 10 s
  - Time step: dt = 0.025 ms (or platform default)
  - Inputs: Poisson background input to excitatory/inhibitory populations; episodic patterned stimulation (e.g., 100–200 ms pulses) to simulate sensory/event-related drive.
  - Trials: 10 seeds per condition
- **Outputs**
  - Spike times, membrane potentials (sampled subset), population synaptic currents (for LFP proxy), power spectra (theta 4–12 Hz, gamma 30–80 Hz), synchrony metrics, firing rate distributions, E/I current ratios.

---

## Proposed analysis plan (what we’ll compare)
- Per-population mean firing rate and firing rate variance (aging vs TBI vs control)
- Spike raster and peri-event time histograms to visualize responses to stimuli
- LFP-like power spectral density — compare theta and gamma power changes
- Network synchrony / cross-correlation and average pairwise coherence
- Changes in effective connectivity (synapse counts and strengths pre/post modifications)
- Sensitivity analysis: effect of lesion severity or synapse loss magnitude on these metrics

---

## Next steps I can take for you

A. Draft simulation configurations (SimulationCampaign + Simulations) for the three conditions (control adult, aged, adult+TBI) with the default parameters above. I can prepare parameter sweeps for TBI/aging severity. (I will use the state manipulation tools to produce draft configs; after that you or the frontend will generate and run simulations.)

brain_structures_movement.user: What are the structures of the brain mainly involved in movement?
brain_structures_movement.ai_response:
## Overview — Main brain structures involved in movement

Movement is produced by a distributed network that spans cortex, subcortex, brainstem and spinal cord. Below are the principal structures and their key roles.

### Cortical regions
- **Primary motor cortex (M1)**  
  - Location: precentral gyrus.  
  - Role: execution and fine control of voluntary movements; sends direct corticospinal output to spinal motor circuits.  
- **Premotor cortex**  
  - Role: planning and selection of movements in response to external cues; coordinates proximal and trunk muscles.  
- **Supplementary motor area (SMA)**  
  - Role: internally generated movement planning, sequencing, and coordination of bimanual actions.  
- **Posterior parietal cortex**  
  - Role: sensorimotor integration — transforms sensory information (vision, proprioception) into movement plans.

### Basal ganglia (subcortical nuclei)
- **Striatum (caudate + putamen)**, **globus pallidus (internal/external segments)**, **subthalamic nucleus**, **substantia nigra (pars compacta and pars reticulata)**  
  - Role: action selection, initiation and suppression of competing motor programs, scaling of movement amplitude; critical for habitual and learned actions.  
  - Clinical relevance: dysfunction produces hypokinetic (Parkinson’s) or hyperkinetic (Huntington’s, hemiballismus) movement disorders.

### Cerebellum
- **Cerebellar cortex and deep nuclei (dentate, interposed, fastigial)**  
  - Role: coordination, timing, error correction and motor learning; adjusts ongoing movements using sensory feedback and internal models.  
  - Clinical relevance: lesions cause ataxia, dysmetria, intention tremor.

### Thalamus (motor nuclei)
- **Ventral lateral (VL) and ventral anterior (VA) nuclei**  
  - Role: relay and integrate cerebellar and basal ganglia output to motor and premotor cortex; important in movement initiation and coordination.

### Brainstem motor centers
- **Red nucleus**  
  - Role: contributes to limb movement coordination (rubrospinal pathway—more significant in some non-human species).  
- **Vestibular nuclei**  
  - Role: balance and posture; coordinate vestibulospinal reflexes.  
- **Reticular formation (medial and lateral reticulospinal systems)**  
  - Role: posture, tone, locomotor pattern modulation, and widespread facilitation/inhibition of motor output.

### Spinal cord and lower motor neurons
- **Spinal interneurons and alpha motor neurons**  
  - Role: final common pathway—generate muscle contractions. Spinal circuits implement reflexes, central pattern generators (CPGs) for rhythmic movements (e.g., walking).  
- **Corticospinal tract**  
  - Role: descending pathway (originating largely in M1 and premotor areas) that provides direct control of spinal motor circuits, especially important for skilled hand movements in primates.

### Peripheral motor system
- **Neuromuscular junction and muscle fibers**  
  - Role: convert motoneuron activity into force and movement.

## How these components interact (brief)
- Cortical motor areas plan and initiate voluntary actions → basal ganglia and cerebellum gate, scale and refine those plans → thalamus relays processed signals back to cortex → descending tracts (corticospinal, corticobulbar, reticulospinal, vestibulospinal) convey commands to spinal circuits → spinal interneurons and alpha motor neurons produce muscle activation; sensory feedback updates the loop for correction (cerebellum particularly important for real-time error correction).

## Clinical/functional notes (high level)
- Damage to **M1** → weakness or paralysis and loss of fine motor control.  
- Basal ganglia disorders → movement initiation and control problems (rigidity, tremor, involuntary movements).  
- Cerebellar damage → incoordination without weakness (ataxia).  
- Spinal cord injury → loss of voluntary movement below lesion; reflex changes.

If you’d like, I can:
- Provide a labeled diagram or figure URLs for these structures,  
- Give a more detailed circuit-level description (e.g., direct/indirect basal ganglia pathways, cerebellar microcircuit), or  
- Summarize clinical signs and localizing rules for each structure. Which would you prefer?

entitysdk_library.ai_response:
I'll look up the package ID for entitysdk, then fetch its documentation.## Overview

**entitysdk** — **ID: /openbraininstitute/entitysdk**

**entitysdk** is a Python SDK for interacting with the platform's **entitycore** service. It provides a type-safe, high-level interface to create, read, update and manage neuroscience-related entities and assets (e.g., cell morphologies, electrophysiology, simulations, models, circuits) from Python notebooks or scripts.

Source summary (from the OBI docs):
- \entitysdk is a Python library for interacting with the entitycore service, providing a type-safe and intuitive interface for managing scientific entities and their associated assets."
- Developed and published by the Open Brain Institute.

---

## Key capabilities

- Initialize an authenticated client for the entitycore API.
- Work with typed models for common neuroscience entities (e.g., **CellMorphology**, **Species**, **Strain**, etc.).
- Token management helpers for environment-based, static, or dynamic tokens.
- Example notebooks and many code snippets showing common usage patterns.

---

## Installation

```bash
pip install entitysdk
```

---

## Typical initialization patterns (examples)

- Initialize with a project context and token (staging example):

```python
from uuid import UUID
from entitysdk import Client, ProjectContext
from obi_auth import get_token

# Get authentication token
token = get_token(environment=\staging")

# Create project context
project_context = ProjectContext(
    project_id=UUID(\12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012"),
    virtual_lab_id=UUID(\87654321-4321-4321-4321-210987654321")
)

# Initialize client with environment
client = Client(
    project_context=project_context,
    environment=\staging",
    token_manager=token
)
```

- Initialize with a custom API URL or token string:

```python
# With a custom API URL and direct token
```

platform_purpose_applications.ai_response:
I'll fetch platform documentation to answer precisely. I'll query the platform docs now.## Summary — What the platform is for

The Open Brain Platform (OBP / OBI-one ecosystem) is a cloud-based, provenance-aware environment for building, exploring, configuring and running biophysically detailed neuroscience models and simulations. Its core purpose is to let users discover and reuse experimental data (morphologies, electrophysiology, densities), construct computational models (ion channels, E- and ME-models, synaptomes, circuits), run simulation workflows (single neurons, paired neurons, small microcircuits), and inspect simulation results — all while tracking provenance and enabling parameter scans.

Key platform capabilities (from the documentation):
- Explore experimental data and model components (morphologies, electrophysiology, densities).
- Build and inspect models (ion-channel workflows, neuron skeletonization, synaptomes, E-/ME-models).
- Configure and run simulations (single, paired, small microcircuits), with schema-driven configuration, campaign generation, and status tracking.
- Perform parameter scans and validated workflow runs.
- Store and expose provenance and metadata for reproducibility.
- Provide notebooks, examples, and an API (OBI-one workflows / FastAPI) for reproducible analyses.

## Who it is designed for

The platform is designed for neuroscience and computational neuroscience practitioners who need integrated data → model → simulation workflows. Typical user types (explicitly supported by the product documentation and feature set):

- **Experimental neuroscientists** — to explore and reuse morphologies, electrophysiology, densities and link experimental data to models.
- **Computational neuroscientists / modelers** — to build ion channel models, skeletonize neurons, assemble E-/ME-models and synaptomes, configure parameter scans and run simulations.
- **Data scientists and simulation engineers** — to run campaign-based simulations, analyse outputs, and integrate with analysis notebooks.
- **Project teams / labs** — to share public vs. project-restricted artifacts, manage provenance and collaborate on reproducible workflows.
- **Educators and trainees** — to use example notebooks and curated workflows (tutorials and examples are part of the documentation).

(These roles are reflected in the product pages describing Explore Data, OBI-one workflows, simulation UIs, notebooks and provenance features.)

## Real‑world applications (concrete examples supported by the platform docs)

The documentation lists concrete workflows and use-cases the platform supports; representative real-world applications include:

- **Analyzing neuron morphologies and preparing neurons for simulation**
  - Use neuron skeletonization workflows to process morphologies and extract skeleton representations and morphometrics for downstream modeling.

- **Building and validating ion channel models**
  - Configure, parameterize and validate ion channel models; integrate channels into neuron models and run channel-level parameter scans.

- **Studying synaptic transmission and paired-neuron dynamics**
  - Configure paired-neuron simulations to set up synaptic connections and study synaptic transmission dynamics between two neurons.

- **Running small microcircuit simulations and motif analysis**
  - Configure, generate campaigns and run small microcircuit simulations (3–20 neurons) to investigate circuit motifs, intrinsic/extrinsic connectivity, and to compare parameter combinations.

- **Parameter scans and model exploration for hypothesis testing**
  - Use standardized workflows and campaign generation to sweep parameters and compare resulting simulations for mechanistic hypothesis testing.

- **Reproducible simulation pipelines and provenance tracking**
  - Store configuration, simulation campaigns, executions and results with provenance so experiments can be reproduced and audited.

- **Data discovery and reuse**
  - Browse curated experimental records (morphologies, electrophysiology, densities), filter and download data for integration into models.

- **Education, training and method development**
  - Use example notebooks, tutorials and FastAPI-backed workflows (OBI-one) to teach modeling approaches and prototype new methods.

## Supported outputs and workflows (high level)

- Model building: ion-channel workflows, neuron skeletonization, synaptome construction.
- Simulation types: single-neuron, paired-neuron, small microcircuit; generation → execution → result retrieval workflow.
- Tools for analysis: notebooks, downloadable simulation files (voltage traces, spike times, connectivity), and an API for automation.
- Provenance: campaign → simulations → simulation results tracked for reproducibility.

## Short checklist — when to use the platform

- You want to combine experimental morphologies/ephys with biophysical models and run reproducible simulations.
- You need standardized workflows for building ion channels, skeletonizing neurons, or assembling paired/microcircuit simulations.
- You require campaign-based parameter scans and tracked provenance for simulation campaigns and results.
- You plan to share models/data within a project while keeping provenance and reproducibility.

build_fly_brain.ai_response:
I'll fetch platform documentation about circuit building and relevant product guides. I'll search the OBI documentation (documentationProduct) for guidance.## Plan
I'll give a step‑by‑step workflow (with recommended OBI products and documentation references) you can follow on the Open Brain Platform to build a digital fly brain from existing connectome datasets. I’ll also list required data types, how to prepare and ingest them, recommended scales and modeling choices, and practical tips for validation and simulation.

---

## Quick references (platform docs used)
- **Explore Data Guide** — how to find, inspect and upload data in the platform (Explore product).  
- **OBI-one: Small Circuit Simulations** — programmatic details for generating circuit simulation configs and running small microcircuit simulations with SONATA.  
- **Circuit Simulation (web interface & tips)** — web UI workflow for configuring small microcircuit simulations, campaign generation and running simulations.  
- **Neuron Skeletonization** — preprocessing morphologies, skeleton extraction, and preparing neurons for simulation.  
- **Build Ion Channel Models** — workflows for configuring ion channels and validating channel models.  
- **Contribute and Fix Data** — workflows for uploading, validating and fixing contributed data.  

(These documents are the sources I used to assemble the workflow and map platform capabilities to the fly connectome problem.)

---

## High-level approach
1. Gather and standardize the fly connectome data (neurons, morphologies, synapses, metadata).  
2. Convert and curate into platform-supported artifacts (morphologies, synaptome / SONATA circuits, ion channel / biophys properties).  
3. Upload/ingest artifacts into a project (use Contribute workflows and Explore).  
4. Assemble circuits at the desired scale (small circuits, regions, or system-level circuits) using SONATA circuit format and OBI-one tools.  
5. Validate and refine (skeletonization, channel calibration).  
6. Generate simulation campaigns (OBI-one circuit simulation tools / web UI).  
7. Run simulations and analyze outputs; iterate.

---

## Required data types and what they map to on the platform
- Neuron reconstructions (3D morphologies; SWC, NeuroMorpho, or skeletons) → platform Morphology artifacts; use Neuron Skeletonization workflows to extract/clean skeletons and prepare for ME-models.
- Electrophysiological recordings or estimated membrane properties → electrical cell models / ion channel parameter sets (use Build Ion Channel Model workflows if you need to fit channels).
- Synapse list / connectome (pre/post neuron IDs, locations, synapse types, weights, delays) → Synaptome / SONATA edge populations (connections); this is the backbone for circuits and simulations.
- Cell type annotations and metadata (mtype, layer / neuropil, neurotransmitter) → node population properties used for filtering and building node_sets.
- Spatial registration (atlas coordinates or neuropil mapping) → used by the Explore browser to select regions and by circuit staging.
- Any imaging / EM volumes (optional) → for provenance and linking.

---

## Detailed step‑by‑step workflow

### 1) Inventory the available fly connectome datasets
- Collect sources: EM connectome exports, neuromorphological reconstructions, cell-type tables, synapse CSV/HDF5.
- Decide target scale: full fly brain (very large; likely a system/whole_brain circuit), specific neuropil (region), or modular microcircuits (small circuits up to 20 biophysical neurons) to prototype first.

Why start small: OBI-one supports “small microcircuit” simulations (3–20 biophysical neurons) with mature tooling for configuration and simulation. Use them for iterative validation before scaling up.

### 2) Preprocess morphologies and skeletons
- Use the **Neuron Skeletonization** workflows to:
  - Clean/repair reconstructions
  - Extract skeletons and morphometric metadata
  - Ensure file formats and metadata match platform expectations
- Produce cleaned morphology artifacts suitable for ME-model building.

Documentation pointers:
- Neuron skeletonization docs show steps and example notebooks for processing morphologies and preparing them for simulation.

### 3) Prepare/standardize synapse and connectivity data into SONATA
- Convert the connectome synapse list into SONATA circuit format (node_populations, edge_populations, and node_sets).
  - Edge populations should include pre/post IDs, synapse location info, synapse type (exc./inh.), and weights/delays where available.
- If you plan to run small microcircuit simulations first, create SONATA circuits for those subcircuits (≤ 20 biophysical neurons) to start.

Why SONATA:
- OBI-one CircuitSimulation tools expect SONATA circuit configs. The OBI-one documentation describes how Circuit and CircuitFromID are handled and staged.

### 4) Define node sets and population properties
- Use node property columns (cell types, neuropil membership, neurotransmitter) to create useful node_sets:
  - Examples: AllExcitatory, AllInhibitory, ID-based sets for small experiments, PropertyNeuronSet to select by mtype.
- The **obione-circuitmetrics** outputs will list property_names and property_unique_values; these are the fields you can use for filtering when analyzing connectivity and building node_sets.

### 5) Upload artifacts to your project in the platform
- Use the **Explore Data Guide** and **Contribute and Fix Data** workflows to:
  - Upload morphologies, synaptome/SONATA files, and metadata to a project (Project mode).
  - Validate and fix metadata and file-format issues using the provided upload wizards and validation steps.
- Keep provenance (source DOI, dataset versions) in metadata.

Note: The Explore docs describe how to toggle between Public and Project modes, upload items, and retrieve IDs for uploaded artifacts.

### 6) Build or tune electrical models and ion channels
- If you have electrophysiological data, use **Build Ion Channel Models** workflows to:
  - Fit ion channel parameters
  - Create or refine electrical cell models (ME-models) to pair with morphologies
- Use these models to generate ME-models that will be referenced by the SONATA node_populations (or by the Circuit ME-model pipeline).

### 7) Generate circuit metrics and verify connectivity
- Run the **circuit metrics** tool (OBI-one obione-circuitmetrics) on your SONATA circuit to:
  - Inspect node populations, available filtering properties, and node_set names
  - Confirm edge populations and synaptic parameter availability
- Use the connectivity metrics tool (obione-circuitconnectivitymetrics) for connection probabilities and synapse counts once you have the right node/edge groups selected.

These steps let you confirm the structure before attempting simulations.

### 8) Start with small microcircuit simulations (iterative validation)

m_vs_e_model_explanation.user: Explain the difference between m-model and e-model.
m_vs_e_model_explanation.expected_output:
The m-model is a detailed 3D reconstructed or synthesised morphology. A 3D reconstructed morphology is generated by tracing a microscopic images of biological neurons. The images are usally obtained using usally thin slices of tissue plated on a glass. Some reconstructed morphologies are obtained by `skeletonisation` of electron microscopic meshses (e.g. MiCRONS morphology meshes). The reconstructed morphologies usually undergo a pipeline to correct and fix for problems which can cause issues during simualtions. Synthesised morphologies are generated by topological synthesis using features from multiple morphologies. 
An e-model or electrical model is a usually a NEURON simulator-based single neuron model which is constructed by adding ion channel models to an reconstructed or synthesised morphology to generate electrical activity. The e-models are optimised using multi-objective optimisation (e.g. using BluePyEModel or BluePyOpt) to generate specific firing patterns as seen in real electrophysiological recordings. These models are also validated by using protocols and electrical features or e-features (e.g. using eFEL python library) which were not used for optimisation. E-models form a template for build more such neuron models by combining with morphologies (m-model) to generate me-models.  The main differences between an e-model and me-model is that e-models are optimised and non-simulatable (on the platform). Whereas me-models are formed by combining m-model and e-model, and can be simulated on the platform. The me-models are not optimised for any cobination of m-model and e-model.

  - Evidence: the Synaptome glossary entry describes a synaptome as a combination of “a neuronal morphology, an electrical model and a set of incoming synaptic connections.”

- **ME-model (morphoelectric model)**: **an ME-model is an E-model combined with the neuron’s morphology**. ME-models pair the electrical model with a reconstruction/geometry so they can be simulated as realistic single-cell models on the platform (example: run with BlueCelluLab).  
  - Evidence: the ME-model glossary entry: “An ME-model, or morphoelectric model, is a combination of an E-model with relevant morphology.”

Week 1 — Orientation

Week 2 — Define a focused research question

Week 4 — Build a minimal model or analysis
- If an astrocyte ME-model exists, run a single-cell simulation or analyze molecular parameter distributions.

Week 5–6 — Iteration and validation
